prompt:
Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A company's customer service department handles 1,000 calls per day with
an average wait time of 8 minutes. Management wants to reduce wait time
to 4 minutes and plans to double the number of agents. Before approving
this expensive hiring plan, what question should they be asking instead?
What alternative approaches might achieve the same goal at lower cost?
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":11705,"duration_api_ms":11644,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"problem_type\": \"Systems/operations thinking + cost optimization\", \"strategy\": \"Reframe before solving — question the assumed solution, then decompose the actual levers\", \"hardest_part\": \"Resisting the intuitive answer (more agents = less wait) and identifying that the problem statement contains an embedded assumption worth challenging\", \"solution\": \"The question they should ask first: 'What is actually causing the 8-minute wait?' Doubling agents assumes the bottleneck is agent capacity, but that may not be true. Key diagnostic questions: (1) What is current agent utilization? If agents are only 40% utilized, adding more does nothing. (2) What percentage of wait time is queue vs. handle time? Long handle times mean more agents help less than expected. (3) Are calls distributed unevenly across the day? Peak-hour congestion might be solved by scheduling shifts differently, not hiring. Alternative approaches at lower cost: (a) Shift scheduling optimization — match staffing to call volume curves, eliminate under/overstaffing by time of day; (b) Reduce average handle time — better tooling, knowledge base, or call routing reduces how long each call takes, which frees agents faster; (c) Deflection — IVR self-service, chatbots, or callback options reduce calls that reach agents at all; (d) First-call resolution improvement — if callbacks/repeat calls are high, fixing root causes reduces total call volume; (e) Skills-based routing — routing calls to the right agent first time reduces transfers and repeat holds. The core insight: wait time = f(arrival rate, handle time, agent count, scheduling). Doubling agents addresses one variable. The others may be cheaper to fix and may have been ignored entirely.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"3b4ed49d-fe98-4d5f-871e-acd4edc71d3c","total_cost_usd":0.048289349999999995,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":10417,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":391,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":10417},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":391,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":10417,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.048289349999999995,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"22dfdf46-3867-4f1a-9f45-4f0fd6e4e2e1"}

parsed.strategy: Reframe before solving — question the assumed solution, then decompose the actual levers
parsed.hardest_part: Resisting the intuitive answer (more agents = less wait) and identifying that the problem statement contains an embedded assumption worth challenging
parsed.solution: The question they should ask first: 'What is actually causing the 8-minute wait?' Doubling agents assumes the bottleneck is agent capacity, but that may not be true. Key diagnostic questions: (1) What is current agent utilization? If agents are only 40% utilized, adding more does nothing. (2) What percentage of wait time is queue vs. handle time? Long handle times mean more agents help less than expected. (3) Are calls distributed unevenly across the day? Peak-hour congestion might be solved by scheduling shifts differently, not hiring. Alternative approaches at lower cost: (a) Shift scheduling optimization — match staffing to call volume curves, eliminate under/overstaffing by time of day; (b) Reduce average handle time — better tooling, knowledge base, or call routing reduces how long each call takes, which frees agents faster; (c) Deflection — IVR self-service, chatbots, or callback options reduce calls that reach agents at all; (d) First-call resolution improvement — if callbacks/repeat calls are high, fixing root causes reduces total call volume; (e) Skills-based routing — routing calls to the right agent first time reduces transfers and repeat holds. The core insight: wait time = f(arrival rate, handle time, agent count, scheduling). Doubling agents addresses one variable. The others may be cheaper to fix and may have been ignored entirely.
